Principles of Reliability Engineering

This essay sketches a general philosophy of site reliability engineering. It is
a vendor-neutral, person-neutral reference and does not describe any specific
individual's career or opinions.

ERROR BUDGETS OVER PERFECTION

Perfect reliability is the wrong target. Past a point, each additional nine of
availability costs more than it returns, and it slows the feature work that
justifies the service existing at all. A service-level objective paired with an
error budget makes the trade-off explicit: as long as the budget is intact, the
team ships; when it is spent, reliability work takes priority until the budget
recovers. The objective is a contract between the people who run the service and
the people who depend on it, not an aspiration nobody enforces.

TOIL IS A TAX, NOT A JOB

Manual, repetitive, automatable work scales linearly with the system while the
team does not. Treat toil as a tax on engineering capacity and budget time to
pay it down. The goal is not zero toil — some manual work is cheaper than the
automation that would replace it — but toil should be measured, capped, and
trending down rather than quietly consuming a rotation.

BLAMELESS CULTURE IS A PREREQUISITE

People who fear punishment hide the information needed to fix systems. A
blameless post-incident review assumes everyone acted reasonably given what they
knew at the time, and asks what about the system made the failure likely. The
output is durable systemic change, not a list of people to be more careful.

STABILIZE BEFORE YOU OPTIMIZE

A system that is on fire cannot be improved, only survived. Restore service
first, understand later. Premature optimization of an unstable system wastes
effort on a moving target and often makes the instability harder to diagnose.

OBSERVABILITY IS NON-NEGOTIABLE

You cannot operate what you cannot see. Metrics, logs, and traces are the three
pillars, but the real test is whether an on-call engineer can answer "what
changed and what is the blast radius" in minutes. Instrument for the questions
you will ask at three in the morning, not the dashboards that look impressive in
a review.
